4753.history: The Norwegian corvette Eglantine served with the Liverpool Escort Force until 29 November 1944. From December 1944 until May 1945 she served off North East Norway, based at Murmansk and Kirkenes. She was purchased by the Norwegian Government in 1946 and renamed Soroy on 10 August 1946. She was then emplyed in fishery protection service. She was sold out of the Navy in August 1956 after 15 years of hard work in war and peace. She was bought by Thor Dahl Whaling company and converted to a whaler with diesel engine at Framnæs Mek.V, Sandefjord. Reconstruction was finished in November 1957 and she was renamed Thorglimt. Last season in Antarctic was 1967/1968. Laid up in Sandefjord from May 1968 to June 1969. Sold to shipbreakers in Grimstad.

Most likely the mine she hit was one of the minefield laid on 21 July 1940 by the British minelaying submarine HMS Rorqual.

4797.history: Former passenger ship of the KNSM. Requisitioned by the Royal Netherlands Navy on 8 November 1940 and subsequently converted to a submarine tender at Dundee. HrMs Colombia (Kapitein ter zee (Capt.) John Louis Karel Hoeke, RNethN) was en-route from East London which she had departed early on 27 February 1943 to the Simonstown Dockyard where she was sheduled to dock in the Selborne graving dock on 2 March 1943. It was intended that following this docking the ship was to proceed to Colombo. On her departure from East London she was escorted by the British corvette HMS Genista (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Pattinson, DSC, RNR) and several aircraft. A heavier surface escort was unfortunately not available. At 0944B/27 the German submarine U-516 fired a spread of three torpedoes from a distance of 1500 metres on a ship near Simonstown, which was identified as a passenger-cargo ship of the Blue Star Line of 10000 tons. Four minutes later, one torpedo struck the Colombia in No.2 hold, just before the bridge. A large column of water arose beside the ship, while the deckplanks were thrown in all directions. All hands immediately abandoned ship, apparently without any casualties and the ship sank after ten minutes in position 33°36'S, 27°29'E. A headcount showed that eight men were missing. Almost 60 survivors in one whaleboat were picked up by an air-sea-rescue launch. The corvette initially launched a counter-attack, but she returned soon and picked up the remaining survivors. Hit by U-boatSunk on 27 Feb 1943 by U-516 (Wiebe).

4863.history: On 10 January 1943 a Japanese plane belonging to the 582nd Naval Air Group, flying ASW patrols in the vicinity of a 5-ship convoy to the south-east of New Britain, position 05º40'S, 152º02'E, spotted a submerged sub approaching the convoy at 1820 hrs (Tokyo time). The plane dropped 3 bombs then contacted destroyer Maikaze which left the convoy to locate and engage the contact, leaving the escort to the Isokaze and Hamakaze (all offsite links). Maikaze depth charge attack evidently was succesfull, since a submarine's bow broke surface at 1845 hrs and was taken under fire by both Maikaze and Isokaze from short range this was witnessed by a pilot of a USAAF bomber returning from a mission over New Guinea. The Argonaut was sunk with no survivors from the 102 officers and men aboard. Lt.Cdr John Reeves Pierce later had a destroyer named after him the Sumner class USS John R. Pierce.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Ralph Edward Hanson, USN (1939 - 13 Jun 1940)
Lt.Cdr. Stephen George Barchet, USN (13 Jun 1940 - 22 Jun 1942)
Lt.Cdr. John Reeves Pierce, USN (22 Jun 1942 - 11 Jan 1943)
